narrative:
President Trump on Tuesday contradicted his Secretary of State Marco Rubio regarding who initiated the recent U.S.-Israel strikes against Iran, claiming he "might have forced Israel's hand" rather than being pressured by Israel to act [1][2][3]. 

## Trump's Account vs. Rubio's Version

When asked directly whether Israel forced the U.S. into launching strikes against Iran, Trump responded: "No, I might have forced their hand" [1][2][6]. He explained that "we were having negotiations with these lunatics and it was my opinion that they were going to attack first" [1][2][6]. Trump stated he "felt strongly" that Iran would attack first and "didn't want that to happen," leading him to potentially force Israel's hand instead [1][2][6].

This directly contradicts Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who said Monday that "the threat from Iran was 'imminent'" because the U.S. knew Iran would retaliate if attacked by Israel [2]. Rubio had stated: "There absolutely was an imminent threat... we knew that if Iran was attacked, and we believed they would be attacked, that they would immediately come after us" [2].

## Military Impact and Iran's Response

Trump claimed the strikes have had "a very very powerful impact because virtually everything they have has been knocked out now" with Iran's "missile count going way down" [1][3][6]. He stated that Iran "no longer have air protection" and "no longer have any detection facilities at all left" [6].

Trump expressed surprise at Iran's retaliation pattern, noting they were "hitting countries that were, you know, let's call them neutral, right? They lived together for a long time" [1][2][6]. He said these previously neutral countries "are all fighting against them and fighting strongly against them" now [1][3][6]. Trump emphasized that Iran was "hitting only civilian places, hotels, and apartment buildings" while the U.S. was "hitting them where it is much more appropriate" [6].

## Casualties and Leadership Strikes

Trump provided specific casualty figures, stating that "49 people were taken out in the first hit" and referenced "another hit today on the new leadership" that "looks like that was pretty substantial also" [6]. He claimed Iran had "violently killed" 35,000 protesters "over the last 3 weeks" and that "the leader of the pack is gone" [6].

## International Tensions

The strikes have created diplomatic friction with allies. Trump criticized Spain and the UK for their lack of cooperation, with reports indicating Spain refused to let the U.S. use jointly operated bases for Iran strikes [3]. Trump also "chides Starmer for unwillingness to back US strikes on Iran" [3].

## Congressional Oversight

Congress is scheduled to be briefed on the Iran strikes "ahead of possible vote over president's war powers," with "top congressional Democrats demand[ing] specific answers from Trump officials" [3].
